main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Network architecture with two components: backbone for invariant features and adapters for task-specific details","Repurposing adapters as feature modifiers for incremental learning","Co-training both backbone and adapters with a regularization term to encourage shared feature learning","Integration with existing incremental learning methods such as Learning without Forgetting (LwF) and EWC"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning","Balancing stability and plasticity (stability-plasticity dilemma) in incremental/lifelong learning","Efficient memory usage in incremental learning scenarios with limited storage or privacy constraints"],"datasets":["CIFAR-100","ImageNet"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy (CIFAR-100 and ImageNet)","value":"Adapter-based methods consistently outperform non-adapter counterparts across various task orders and data scales"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel use of adapters as feature modifiers for capturing task-specific details in incremental learning","Co-training both backbone and adapters, unlike prior work that keeps the backbone frozen","Introduces a regularization term to encourage the backbone to learn shared features","Demonstrates that adapter-based methods improve both plasticity and stability, addressing the stability-plasticity dilemma","Shows that the approach integrates seamlessly with established methods like LwF and EWC and outperforms non-adapter baselines"]}

methods: Network architecture with two components: backbone for invariant features and adapters for task-specific details
Repurposing adapters as feature modifiers for incremental learning
Co-training both backbone and adapters with a regularization term to encourage shared feature learning
Integration with existing incremental learning methods such as Learning without Forgetting (LwF) and EWC
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning
Balancing stability and plasticity (stability-plasticity dilemma) in incremental/lifelong learning
Efficient memory usage in incremental learning scenarios with limited storage or privacy constraints

results.value: Adapter-based methods consistently outperform non-adapter counterparts across various task orders and data scales

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel use of adapters as feature modifiers for capturing task-specific details in incremental learning
Co-training both backbone and adapters, unlike prior work that keeps the backbone frozen
Introduces a regularization term to encourage the backbone to learn shared features
Demonstrates that adapter-based methods improve both plasticity and stability, addressing the stability-plasticity dilemma
Shows that the approach integrates seamlessly with established methods like LwF and EWC and outperforms non-adapter baselines

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["iTAML (Incremental Task-Agnostic Meta-Learning) algorithm","Task-agnostic meta-update rule to maintain equilibrium between old and new knowledge","Separation of generic feature extraction module from task-specific classifier","Momentum-based strategy for meta-update to avoid forgetting","Sampling rate selection approach for lower-bound sample estimation during inference"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning","Maintaining balance between plasticity and stability in class-incremental learning","Adapting meta-learning to incremental learning without task-specific feedback","Data imbalance in incremental learning due to majority samples from new tasks"],"datasets":["ImageNet","CIFAR100","MNIST","SVHN","MS-Celeb"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy improvement on CIFAR100 (10 incremental tasks)","value":"21.3% boost over state of the art"},{"metric":"Classification accuracy improvement on ImageNet","value":"19.1% absolute gain"},{"metric":"Classification accuracy improvement on MS-Celeb","value":"7.4% absolute gain"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new task-agnostic meta-learning algorithm (iTAML) that predicts the task and final class automatically in incremental learning settings","Introduces a momentum-based meta-update strategy to effectively avoid catastrophic forgetting","Presents a new sampling rate selection approach for determining the lower-bound of samples needed for meta-update during inference","Demonstrates significant performance gains over state-of-the-art methods on large-scale and challenging datasets in class-incremental learning"]}

methods: iTAML (Incremental Task-Agnostic Meta-Learning) algorithm
Task-agnostic meta-update rule to maintain equilibrium between old and new knowledge
Separation of generic feature extraction module from task-specific classifier
Momentum-based strategy for meta-update to avoid forgetting
Sampling rate selection approach for lower-bound sample estimation during inference
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning
Maintaining balance between plasticity and stability in class-incremental learning
Adapting meta-learning to incremental learning without task-specific feedback
Data imbalance in incremental learning due to majority samples from new tasks

results.metric: Classification accuracy improvement on CIFAR100 (10 incremental tasks)
results.value: 21.3% boost over state of the art
19.1% absolute gain
7.4% absolute gain

novelty_claims: Proposes a new task-agnostic meta-learning algorithm (iTAML) that predicts the task and final class automatically in incremental learning settings
Introduces a momentum-based meta-update strategy to effectively avoid catastrophic forgetting
Presents a new sampling rate selection approach for determining the lower-bound of samples needed for meta-update during inference
Demonstrates significant performance gains over state-of-the-art methods on large-scale and challenging datasets in class-incremental learning

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Taxonomy and overview of state-of-the-art continual learning methods","Novel framework for dynamically determining the stability-plasticity trade-off in continual learners","Comprehensive experimental comparison of 11 state-of-the-art continual learning methods","Baselines for continual learning"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in neural networks during sequential learning","Stability-plasticity dilemma in continual learning","Lack of comprehensive experimental comparison of continual learning methods","Fair evaluation of continual learning methods in task incremental classification settings"],"datasets":["Tiny Imagenet","iNaturalist (large-scale, unbalanced)","RecogSeq (sequence of recognition datasets)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Memory usage","Computation time","Storage requirements"],"results":[{"metric":"Impact of task order","value":"Insignificant impact on performance"},{"metric":"Generalization on Tiny Imagenet","value":"All representative methods generalize well"},{"metric":"Generalization on iNaturalist and RecogSeq","value":"Only parameter isolation methods withstand generalizing to these challenging settings"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel framework to dynamically determine the stability-plasticity trade-off for continual learners using only current task data","Provides a comprehensive experimental comparison of 11 state-of-the-art continual learning methods on both small-scale and large-scale, unbalanced datasets","Presents a taxonomy and extensive overview of continual learning methods specifically for task incremental classification","Studies the influence of model capacity, weight decay, dropout regularization, and task order on continual learning performance","Qualitatively compares methods in terms of memory, computation time, and storage requirements"]}

methods: Taxonomy and overview of state-of-the-art continual learning methods

results.value: Insignificant impact on performance

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel framework to dynamically determine the stability-plasticity trade-off for continual learners using only current task data

Qualitatively compares methods in terms of memory, computation time, and storage requirements

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Interference-Free Low-Rank Adaptation (InfLoRA)","Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with interference-free subspace design"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning","Interference of new tasks on old tasks in PEFT-based continual learning","Trade-off between stability (retaining old knowledge) and plasticity (adapting to new tasks) in continual learning"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["InfLoRA introduces a new PEFT method that injects a small number of parameters to reparameterize pretrained weights, enabling fine-tuning within a subspace.","InfLoRA explicitly designs the adaptation subspace to eliminate interference of new tasks on old tasks, improving the trade-off between stability and plasticity in continual learning.","InfLoRA outperforms existing state-of-the-art continual learning methods on multiple datasets."]}

methods: Interference-Free Low-Rank Adaptation (InfLoRA)
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with interference-free subspace design
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning
Interference of new tasks on old tasks in PEFT-based continual learning
Trade-off between stability (retaining old knowledge) and plasticity (adapting to new tasks) in continual learning
novelty_claims: InfLoRA introduces a new PEFT method that injects a small number of parameters to reparameterize pretrained weights, enabling fine-tuning within a subspace.
InfLoRA explicitly designs the adaptation subspace to eliminate interference of new tasks on old tasks, improving the trade-off between stability and plasticity in continual learning.
InfLoRA outperforms existing state-of-the-art continual learning methods on multiple datasets.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["AdapterFusion: a two-stage learning algorithm involving (1) training task-specific adapters and (2) a Fusion layer that combines multiple adapters' representations for transfer learning"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in sequential fine-tuning for transfer learning","Difficulty in balancing datasets and tasks in multi-task learning","Inefficient knowledge transfer across multiple tasks without destructive interference"],"datasets":["16 diverse NLU tasks (including sentiment analysis, commonsense reasoning, paraphrase detection, and recognizing textual entailment)"],"metrics":["Task-specific evaluation metrics (e.g., accuracy, F1 score, depending on the NLU task)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance on 16 NLU tasks (compared to full fine-tuning and multi-task learning)","value":"AdapterFusion outperforms both full fine-tuning and multi-task learning approaches across all evaluated tasks"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of AdapterFusion, a novel two-stage transfer learning strategy that non-destructively combines knowledge from multiple tasks via adapters and a fusion layer","Separation of knowledge extraction (adapter training) and knowledge composition (fusion) to avoid catastrophic forgetting and task balancing issues","Demonstration that AdapterFusion outperforms both full fine-tuning and multi-task adapter training on a diverse set of NLU tasks"]}

methods: AdapterFusion: a two-stage learning algorithm involving (1) training task-specific adapters and (2) a Fusion layer that combines multiple adapters' representations for transfer learning
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in sequential fine-tuning for transfer learning
Difficulty in balancing datasets and tasks in multi-task learning
Inefficient knowledge transfer across multiple tasks without destructive interference

Representational shift in latent space when learning new tasks incrementally, leading to forgetting of previous tasks

ELI can be used as a plug-and-play module with existing incremental learning methods, requiring no changes to their methodology

methods: TAIL (Task-specific Adapters for Imitation Learning) framework
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques: Bottleneck Adapters, P-Tuning (Prefix Tuning), Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
Incorporation of lightweight adapter modules into pretrained decision-making models
Comparison of PEFT techniques with standard adaptation baselines in imitation learning
problems: Efficient adaptation of large pretrained models to new control tasks in robotics and control domains
Data-efficient and continual adaptation for new control tasks with limited demonstration data
Avoiding catastrophic forgetting and overfitting during adaptation
Reducing computational and memory burden during adaptation
Maintaining performance on old tasks while adapting to new ones
datasets: Large-scale language-conditioned manipulation tasks (specific datasets not named in the provided text)

results.metric: Trainable parameters (TAIL with LoRA vs. full fine-tuning)
results.value: 1% of the trainable parameters of full fine-tuning
1.17% of the size of the original model

results.metric: GPU memory consumption reduction (TAIL vs. full fine-tuning)
results.value: 23% less GPU memory consumption

results.metric: Forward adaptation success rate improvement (TAIL vs. full fine-tuning)
results.value: 22% higher success rate

novelty_claims: Introduction of TAIL, a framework for efficient and continual adaptation of large pretrained models to new control tasks via task-specific adapters.
Comprehensive comparison of parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques (Bottleneck Adapters, P-Tuning, LoRA) in the context of continual imitation learning for control tasks.

Demonstration that TAIL with LoRA achieves superior post-adaptation performance with only 1% of the trainable parameters of full fine-tuning, while reducing memory consumption and avoiding catastrophic forgetting.

Bridging the gap between adaptation techniques in language/vision domains and their application to decision-making/control domains, showing that insights from language models do not always transfer to control tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Learning without Memorizing (LwM) approach","Attention Distillation Loss (L_AD) as an information preserving penalty","Combination of classification loss, distillation loss (L_D), and attention distillation loss (L_AD) for incremental learning"],"problems":["Incremental learning of object classes without storing data of base classes","Preventing catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning without increasing memory footprint","Maintaining performance on both base and new classes in a single classifier"],"datasets":["iILSVRC-small","iCIFAR-100"],"metrics":["Overall accuracy of base and incrementally learned classes"],"results":[{"metric":"Overall accuracy (iILSVRC-small)","value":"LwM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (exact values not provided)"},{"metric":"Overall accuracy (iCIFAR-100)","value":"LwM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (exact values not provided)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes an attention-based approach (LwM) for incremental learning that does not require storing any data of base classes.","Introduces Attention Distillation Loss (L_AD) to constrain the divergence of attention maps between teacher and student models, preserving knowledge of base classes.","LwM leverages gradient flow information via attention maps, which is not considered in prior methods.","Demonstrates that adding L_AD to the distillation loss improves performance over state-of-the-art on standard incremental learning benchmarks."]}

methods: Learning without Memorizing (LwM) approach
Attention Distillation Loss (L_AD) as an information preserving penalty
Combination of classification loss, distillation loss (L_D), and attention distillation loss (L_AD) for incremental learning
problems: Incremental learning of object classes without storing data of base classes
Preventing catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning without increasing memory footprint
Maintaining performance on both base and new classes in a single classifier
metrics: Overall accuracy of base and incrementally learned classes

LwM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (exact values not provided)

novelty_claims: Proposes an attention-based approach (LwM) for incremental learning that does not require storing any data of base classes.
Introduces Attention Distillation Loss (L_AD) to constrain the divergence of attention maps between teacher and student models, preserving knowledge of base classes.
LwM leverages gradient flow information via attention maps, which is not considered in prior methods.
Demonstrates that adding L_AD to the distillation loss improves performance over state-of-the-art on standard incremental learning benchmarks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Global distillation loss (global distillation)","Three-step learning scheme: (i) training a teacher for the current task, (ii) distilling knowledge from previous model, current teacher, and their ensemble, (iii) fine-tuning to avoid overfitting to current task","Confidence-based sampling method for leveraging unlabeled external data"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in lifelong/class-incremental learning with deep neural networks","Effective use of unlabeled data in the wild for class-incremental learning","Overfitting to the most recent task in incremental learning"],"datasets":["CIFAR","ImageNet"],"metrics":["Accuracy","Forgetting"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy (ImageNet, with external dataset)","value":"15.8% higher than state-of-the-art (E2E)"},{"metric":"Forgetting (ImageNet, with external dataset)","value":"46.5% less than state-of-the-art (E2E)"},{"metric":"Accuracy (ImageNet, without external dataset)","value":"4.8% higher than state-of-the-art (E2E)"},{"metric":"Forgetting (ImageNet, without external dataset)","value":"6.0% less than state-of-the-art (E2E)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new global distillation loss that distills knowledge across all previous tasks, not just task-wise as in prior works.","Introduces a three-step learning scheme to improve global distillation and avoid overfitting to the current task.","Develops a confidence-based sampling method to effectively leverage large streams of unlabeled data in the wild for class-incremental learning.","Demonstrates that leveraging unlabeled external data with the proposed methods significantly improves performance and reduces forgetting compared to state-of-the-art."]}

methods: Global distillation loss (global distillation)

Three-step learning scheme: (i) training a teacher for the current task, (ii) distilling knowledge from previous model, current teacher, and their ensemble, (iii) fine-tuning to avoid overfitting to current task
Confidence-based sampling method for leveraging unlabeled external data
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in lifelong/class-incremental learning with deep neural networks
Effective use of unlabeled data in the wild for class-incremental learning
Overfitting to the most recent task in incremental learning

results.value: 15.8% higher than state-of-the-art (E2E)
46.5% less than state-of-the-art (E2E)
4.8% higher than state-of-the-art (E2E)
6.0% less than state-of-the-art (E2E)

novelty_claims: Proposes a new global distillation loss that distills knowledge across all previous tasks, not just task-wise as in prior works.
Introduces a three-step learning scheme to improve global distillation and avoid overfitting to the current task.
Develops a confidence-based sampling method to effectively leverage large streams of unlabeled data in the wild for class-incremental learning.
Demonstrates that leveraging unlabeled external data with the proposed methods significantly improves performance and reduces forgetting compared to state-of-the-art.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Learning to Prompt for Continual Learning (L2P): a framework that learns a pool of prompts (small learnable parameters) to instruct a frozen pre-trained model for sequential tasks, using a key-value memory space and an instance-wise query mechanism to select relevant prompts without requiring task identity at test time"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning when training on non-stationary data distributions","Dependence on rehearsal buffers or known task identity at test time in existing continual learning methods","Need for a succinct and intelligent episodic memory system for continual learning","Enabling task-agnostic continual learning where task identity and boundaries are unknown"],"datasets":["Popular image classification benchmarks (specific datasets not named in the provided content, but likely include standard continual learning datasets such as CIFAR-100, ImageNet, etc.)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy","value":"L2P consistently outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods across all benchmarks; achieves competitive results against rehearsal-based methods even without a rehearsal buffer (exact numbers not provided in the content)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of prompting (prompt-based learning) to continual learning for the first time","Proposing L2P, a continual learning framework that uses a prompt pool as a memory system, enabling learning without rehearsal buffer or task identity at test time","Instance-wise prompt selection mechanism that enables task-agnostic continual learning","Decoupling shared and task-specific knowledge via prompt pool to minimize catastrophic forgetting"]}

methods: Dynamically expandable representation (super-feature) for incremental learning

Achieves new state-of-the-art performance on three class incremental learning benchmarks across a wide range of model complexities

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Dynamically Expandable Network (DEN): a deep network architecture that dynamically expands its capacity during lifelong learning by selectively retraining, adding new units only when necessary, splitting/duplicating units, and timestamping them to prevent semantic drift"],"problems":["Lifelong learning in deep neural networks (continual learning with sequentially arriving tasks)","Catastrophic forgetting (semantic drift) in incremental learning","Efficient and scalable training as network capacity grows","Dynamic determination of network capacity for new tasks"],"datasets":["Multiple public datasets (specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Performance (accuracy or task-specific metric)","Number of parameters used"],"results":[{"metric":"Parameter efficiency","value":"DEN uses only 11.9% - 60.3% of the parameters compared to training a separate network for each task"},{"metric":"Performance improvement over batch model after fine-tuning","value":"DEN outperforms batch model by 0.05%p - 4.8%p"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a deep network architecture (DEN) that dynamically expands its capacity during lifelong learning, adding only the necessary number of units for each new task","DEN selectively retrains only relevant parts of the network for each new task, rather than retraining the whole network or freezing old parameters","Introduces mechanisms such as splitting/duplicating units and timestamping to prevent semantic drift and catastrophic forgetting","DEN achieves similar or better performance than batch models with significantly fewer parameters, and can be used for optimal network structure estimation even in batch settings"]}

methods: Dynamically Expandable Network (DEN): a deep network architecture that dynamically expands its capacity during lifelong learning by selectively retraining, adding new units only when necessary, splitting/duplicating units, and timestamping them to prevent semantic drift
problems: Lifelong learning in deep neural networks (continual learning with sequentially arriving tasks)
Catastrophic forgetting (semantic drift) in incremental learning

results.value: DEN uses only 11.9% - 60.3% of the parameters compared to training a separate network for each task

results.metric: Performance improvement over batch model after fine-tuning
results.value: DEN outperforms batch model by 0.05%p - 4.8%p

novelty_claims: Proposes a deep network architecture (DEN) that dynamically expands its capacity during lifelong learning, adding only the necessary number of units for each new task
DEN selectively retrains only relevant parts of the network for each new task, rather than retraining the whole network or freezing old parameters
Introduces mechanisms such as splitting/duplicating units and timestamping to prevent semantic drift and catastrophic forgetting
DEN achieves similar or better performance than batch models with significantly fewer parameters, and can be used for optimal network structure estimation even in batch settings

methods: Deep Model Consolidation (DMC): a class-incremental learning paradigm that first trains a separate model for new classes, then consolidates the old and new models using a novel double distillation training objective and unlabeled auxiliary data
Double distillation training objective: allows a student model to learn from both old and new teacher models simultaneously using unlabeled auxiliary data
Extension of DMC to one-stage object detectors
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in deep neural networks during class-incremental learning
Bias towards old or new classes in incremental learning without access to original training data
Inefficiency and impracticality of storing old data due to privacy, copyright, or storage constraints
Lack of effective methods for class-incremental learning in the absence of old class data, especially for object detection

results.value: DMC achieves significantly better performance than state-of-the-art methods (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)
DMC outperforms existing approaches in the single-headed IL setting (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel class-incremental learning paradigm (DMC) that does not require access to original training data for old classes
Introduces a double distillation training objective to symmetrically consolidate two models trained on disjoint class sets
Leverages publicly available unlabeled auxiliary data to facilitate unbiased knowledge transfer between models
Demonstrates that DMC can be applied to both image classification and object detection tasks, including modern one-stage detectors, where previous methods are not applicable
Shows that DMC can combine arbitrary pre-trained models with different architectures without access to their original training data

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ATTEMPT (ATTEntional Mixtures of Prompt Tuning): a parameter-efficient multi-task tuning method that uses attentional mixtures of pre-trained soft prompts and a target prompt, with an attention module to interpolate between them for each instance"],"problems":["Parameter-efficient tuning of large language models for multiple tasks","Instability and performance drop in prompt tuning","Leveraging knowledge from high-resource tasks for low-resource or target tasks","Modular and flexible multi-task learning without retraining the full model"],"datasets":["21 diverse NLP datasets (not individually named in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Task performance (e.g., accuracy, F1, etc. as appropriate for each dataset)","Parameter efficiency (number of parameters updated)"],"results":[{"metric":"Parameter efficiency","value":"ATTEMPT updates 2,300 times fewer parameters than full fine-tuning (0.4% of parameters)"},{"metric":"Task performance","value":"Significantly outperforms prompt tuning and matches or outperforms fully fine-tuned or other parameter-efficient tuning approaches using 10x more parameters"},{"metric":"Few-shot learning performance","value":"Outperforms previous work in few-shot settings (4-32 shots)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes ATTEMPT, a new parameter-efficient, modular multi-task tuning method using attentional mixtures of soft prompts","Learns to combine knowledge from multiple source tasks via an attention mechanism, rather than relying on precomputed task relations","Keeps the original language model and source prompts frozen, only updating the target prompt and attention weights","Enables modularity: source prompts can be flexibly added or removed for knowledge transfer","Improves interpretability by generating attention distributions that reveal task similarities","Achieves high performance and parameter efficiency, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art methods with far fewer trainable parameters"]}

methods: ATTEMPT (ATTEntional Mixtures of Prompt Tuning): a parameter-efficient multi-task tuning method that uses attentional mixtures of pre-trained soft prompts and a target prompt, with an attention module to interpolate between them for each instance
problems: Parameter-efficient tuning of large language models for multiple tasks
Instability and performance drop in prompt tuning
Leveraging knowledge from high-resource tasks for low-resource or target tasks
Modular and flexible multi-task learning without retraining the full model
datasets: 21 diverse NLP datasets (not individually named in the provided content)
metrics: Task performance (e.g., accuracy, F1, etc. as appropriate for each dataset)

results.value: ATTEMPT updates 2,300 times fewer parameters than full fine-tuning (0.4% of parameters)
Significantly outperforms prompt tuning and matches or outperforms fully fine-tuned or other parameter-efficient tuning approaches using 10x more parameters
Outperforms previous work in few-shot settings (4-32 shots)

novelty_claims: Proposes ATTEMPT, a new parameter-efficient, modular multi-task tuning method using attentional mixtures of soft prompts
Learns to combine knowledge from multiple source tasks via an attention mechanism, rather than relying on precomputed task relations
Keeps the original language model and source prompts frozen, only updating the target prompt and attention weights
Enables modularity: source prompts can be flexibly added or removed for knowledge transfer
Improves interpretability by generating attention distributions that reveal task similarities
Achieves high performance and parameter efficiency, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art methods with far fewer trainable parameters
